content.full_text:
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 25, 2010


Mr. Tom Sheehy
1946 Wingfield Way
Carmichael, CA 95608

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-155

Dear Mr. Sheehy:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-government employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ We note that this advice is based on the facts provided; The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1. You wish to confirm your understanding that you are barred from lobbying or otherwise seeking to influence government decisions at the State and Consumer Services Agency and any departments, commissions or authorities under its direct budgetary, personnel and operations control, for one year after the date you leave employment in this position.

2. You wish to confirm your understanding that you are barred from lobbying or otherwise seeking to influence government decisions at the Department of Finance and any boards, commissions or authorities you sat on for one year after the date you left employment in this position.

3. You wish to confirm your belief that you do not have a one-year restriction from lobbying the Governor's Office or California Legislature on legislative matters.

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-155
Page No. 2


CONCLUSIONS

1. Your understanding is correct. When you leave your position as Undersecretary of
the State and Consumer Services Agency, you may not, for one year after leaving state service,
act for compensation as representative, agent, or attorney for any person before the State and
Consumer Services Agency or any state administrative agency whose budget, personnel, and
other operations are subject to the direction and control of the State and Consumer Services
Agency. So for example, if you leave your position at the State and Consumer Services Agency
on December 1, 2010, the one-year ban will be in effect until December 1, 2011.

2. Similarly, after your departure from the Department of Finance, the one-year ban
applied and you may not, for one year after leaving that position, act for compensation as
representative, agent, or attorney for any person before the Department of Finance, the boards,
commissions or authorities on which you sat representing for the Director of the Department of
Finance, or any state administrative agency whose budget, personnel, and other operations are
subject to the direction and control of the Department of Finance.5 Because you left your
position at the Department of Finance on January 4, 2010, this one-year ban restriction is in
effect until January 4, 2011.

3. Appearances before the Office of the Governor or the Legislature to influence the
actions of the Governor or the Legislature on legislation are generally not prohibited because, as
Undersecretary for State and Consumer Services Agency, and Chief Deputy Director for Policy
at the Department of Finance, you did not work for the Office of the Governor or the
Legislature.

FACTS

You are writing to seek clarification on the rules, laws and regulations related to
"revolving door" and "cooling off" restrictions and requirements in state government. You are
currently a Governor's appointee at the State and Consumer Services Agency (Undersecretary)
but previously held the Chief Deputy Director position at the California Department of Finance
and Deputy Director before that.

Your appointments chronology is as follows:

•     Appointed Deputy Director for Legislation, Department of Finance December 23, 2006.
•     Appointed Chief Deputy Director for Policy at Department of Finance October 1, 2008.
•     Appointed Undersecretary at the State and Consumer Services Agency January 4, 2010.

_________________________________

5 For individuals moving from one state agency to another, a special provision applies. Section
87406(e)(1) states that the prohibitions of the one-year ban do not apply to any individual who is or becomes an
"officer or employee of another state agency, board, or commission if the appearance or communication is for the
purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action on behalf of the state agency, board, or commission."

File No. A-10-155
Page No. 3

In your role as Chief Deputy at the Department of Finance, which you left on January 4,
2010, you represented the Director of Finance on over 40 boards, commissions and authorities.

In your current role as State and Consumer Services Agency Undersecretary you oversee
14 different departments, commissions and offices that make up the State and Consumer
Services Agency. In the case of some of these entities the State and Consumer Services Agency
exerts direct control over some operations, but in the case of other entities the State and
Consumer Services Agency does not exert any direct management control over budgets or
personnel transactions.

ANALYSIS

There are two provisions in the Act that may restrict your post-governmental
employment. In addition, the Act contains a provision about negotiating prospective
employment.

Negotiating Prospective Employment

The Act prohibits current state officials from taking part in decisions that directly relate
to a prospective employer. Under Section 87407, no public official shall make, participate in
making, or influence a governmental decision that directly relates to a prospective employer
while the official is negotiating or has reached an employment arrangement with that person.
(Section 87407, Regulating 18407.) The Act's provisions concerning negotiating prospective
employment are discussed further in the enclosed fact sheet.

The Permanent Ban

The permanent ban prohibits a state administrative official who leaves state employment
from "switching sides" in a specific proceeding that he or she worked on while in state service.

Sections 87401 and 87402 provide:

"No former state administrative official, after the termination of his or her
employment or term of office, shall for compensation act as agent or attorney for,
or otherwise represent, any other person (other than the State of California) before
any court or state administrative agency or any other officer or employee thereof by
making any formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or written
communication with the intent to influence, in connection with any judicial,
quasi-judicial or other proceeding¹ if both of the following apply:

¹ Section 87400(c) provides: 'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding,
application, request for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge,
accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative
agency, including but not limited to any proceeding governed by Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 11500) of
Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government Code.

File No. A-10-155
Page No. 4

"(a) The State of California is a party or has a direct and substantial interest.

"(b) The proceeding is one in which the former state administrative official participated."

"No former state administrative official, after the termination of his or her employment or term of office shall for compensation aid, advise, counsel, consult or assist in representing any other person (except the State of California) in any proceeding in which the official would be prohibited from appearing under Section 87401."

The State and Consumer Services Agency and the Department of Finance are "state administrative agencies." (Section 87400(a).) According to Section 87400(b), as an officer of the State and Consumer Services Agency and the Department of Finance in a position that had decision-making authority, you are a "state administrative official." Consequently, Sections 87401 and 87402 will prohibit you from representing another person before a court or administrative agency or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting or assisting any other person for compensation in a judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding where the proceeding is one in which you participated; and, the state is a party or has a direct and substantial interest.

You have not asked about any specific proceedings in which you participated at the State and Consumer Services Agency or the Department of Finance. Therefore, we do not analyze the permanent ban in more detail. If you have further questions about specific proceedings, feel free to contact us.

One-Year Ban

The one-year ban prohibits an official from appearing before the agency for which he or she worked for twelve months after leaving state employment. (Section 87406 and Regulations 18746.1-18746.4, copies enclosed.)

Section 87406(d)(1), provides in relevant part:

"No designated employee of a state administrative agency, any officer, employee, or consultant of a state administrative agency who holds a position which entails the making, or participation in the making, of decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest, and no member of a state administrative agency, for a period of one year after leaving office or employment, shall, for compensation, act as agent or attorney for, or otherwise

4 Section 87400(d) provides: "Participated" means to have taken part personally and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation or use of confidential information as an officer or employee, but excluding approval, disapproval or rendering of legal advisory opinions to departmental or agency staff which do not involve a specific party or parties.

File No. A-10-155
Page No. 5

represent, any other person, by making any formal or informal appearance, or by
making any oral or written communication, before any state administrative
agency, or officer or employee thereof, for which he or she worked or represented
during the 12 months before leaving office or employment, if the appearance or
communication is made for the purpose of influencing administrative' or
legislative action,5 or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance,
amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the
sale or purchase of goods or property."

"Influencing legislative or administrative action" includes influencing by any means,
including but not limited to the provision or use of information, statistics, studies, or analyses.
(Section 82032.)

State Administrative Agency You Worked for or Represented

When you leave your position as Undersecretary of the State and Consumer Services
Agency, pursuant to Section 87406(d) you may not act for compensation as a representative,
agent, or attorney before the State and Consumer Services Agency for twelve
months, or before the Department of Finance while that one-year ban is in effect.

Regulation 18746.1(b)(6) provides that the prohibitions of the one-year ban apply to
appearances or communications:

"(C) Made by any former official as specified in subdivision (a)(3)–(a)(5)
before any of the following:

"(i) The state administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, that the
public official worked for or represented as specified in subdivision (a)(3)–(a)(5).
An employee loaned to an agency is deemed to have worked for or represented
that agency.

"(ii) Any state administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, whose
budget, personnel, and other operations are subject to the direction and control of
any agency described in subdivision (b)(6)(C)(i). However, whether an agency is
provided technical assistance or legal advice, or is subject to oversight by another

5 "Administrative action" is defined in Section 82002 as the proposal, drafting, development,
consideration, amendment, enactment, or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation or other action in any
rate-making proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding.

6 For purposes of the prohibition on influencing your former employer's "legislative action," this would
apply to attempts to influence the former employer's action in connection with their position on the drafting,
introduction, consideration, modification, enactment, or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report,
nomination or other matter by the Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select
committee thereof, or by a member or employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity. "Legislative
action" also means the action of the Governor in approving or vetoing any bill. (Section 82037)

File No. A-10-155
Page No. 6

agency pursuant to state law, are not factors to be considered in determining
whether an agency is subject to the direction and control of another."

Thus, generally, a public official's state administrative agency means the agency for
which he or she worked, or any board or commission under that agency's control. However, we
have advised that Section 87406 does not prohibit every former member of the executive branch
from appearing before or communicating with the entire executive branch. For example, in the
Gould Advice Letter, No. A-96-077, we advised that a former director of the Department of
Finance, a distinct state administrative agency, may lobby administrative agencies in the
executive branch which he did not work for during the course of his government service. In the
course of his employment, we concluded that the participated in the budget process as an advisor
to the Governor and the Legislature, but he did not work for or represent those agencies within
the meaning of Section 87406(d)(1). In making a determination as to which agency an employee
works for or represents, the scope of his or her duties is an important factor.

For ex-employees of the Governor's Office, the restrictions are much broader. The
statute provides that the "state administrative agency of a designated employee of the Governor's
office includes any state administrative agency subject to the direction and control of the
Governor." (Section 87406(d)(2).) Following Section 87406(d)(2), for ex-employees of the
Governor's Office, we have advised that the "entire executive branch" is subject to the direction
and control of the Governor. (Davidian Advice Letter, No. A-97-171; Zaramberg Advice Letter,
No. A-92-038; and Sybert Advice Letter, No. I-93-380.)

In your situation, the one-year ban will apply to the State and Consumer Services Agency
and the departments, commissions, and offices whose budget, personnel and other operations are
subject to the control of the State and Consumer Services Agency. The ban on appearances will
apply for one year after you leave the State and Consumer Services Agency.

You note that as State and Consumer Services Agency Undersecretary you oversaw 14
different departments, commissions and offices that make up the State and Consumer Services
Agency.⁷ You state that in the case of some of these entities, the State and Consumer Services
Agency exerts direct control over some operations, but in the case of other entities, the State and
Consumer Services Agency does not exert any direct management control over budgets or
personnel transactions. The one-year ban on appearances will apply to those agencies whose
budget, personnel and other operations are subject to the control of State and Consumer Services
Agency, but not to those who have their own budgets allocated by the Legislature or are

⁷ Included under the umbrella of the State and Consumer Services Agency are the following departments,
commissions, boards and offices: Department of Fair Employment and Housing, Department of Consumer Affairs,
Department of General Services, California Building Standards Commission, Fair Employment and Housing
Commission, Seismic Safety Commission, California Public Employee Retirement System, California State
Teachers' Retirement System, Franchise Tax Board, Victim Compensation and Government Claims Board, State
Personnel Board, California Science Center, Exposition Park, Office of Privacy Protection, California African
American Museum and the Office of the Insurance Advisor.

File No. A-10-155
Page No. 7


independently operated by independent managers (executive director and appointed chairman
and commissioners). (Brown Advice Letter, No. A-08-062.)

In addition, the one-year ban on appearing before the Department of Finance runs for one
year after your departure from that agency. With respect to the Department of Finance, you are
considered to have worked for the boards, commissions and authorities on which you sat
representing the Director of the Department of Finance.

Also, generally, a designated employee of a state administrative agency is not restricted
by Section 87406 from lobbying the Legislature or Governor regarding legislation. (Davidian
Advice Letter, No. A-97-531; Witherspoon Advice Letter, No. A-94-371; and Craven Advice
Letter, No. A-93-057.) In your situation, appearances before the Office of the Governor or the
Legislature to influence legislation are generally not prohibited because, as Undersecretary for
the State and Consumer Services Agency, and Chief Deputy Director for Policy at the
Department of Finance, you did not work for the Office of the Governor or the Legislature.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

[signature: Hyla P. Wagner]

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division


Enclosures

HPW:jgl

sections.question:
1. You wish to confirm your understanding that you are barred from lobbying or otherwise seeking to influence government decisions at the State and Consumer Services Agency and any departments, commissions or authorities under its direct budgetary, personnel and operations control, for one year after the date you leave employment in this position.

2. You wish to confirm your understanding that you are barred from lobbying or otherwise seeking to influence government decisions at the Department of Finance and any boards, commissions or authorities you sat on for one year after the date you left employment in this position.

3. You wish to confirm your belief that you do not have a one-year restriction from lobbying the Governor’s Office or California Legislature on legislative matters.
sections.conclusion:
1. Your understanding is correct. When you leave your position as Undersecretary of the State and Consumer Services Agency, you may not, for one year after leaving state service, act for compensation as representative, agent, or attorney for any person before the State and Consumer Services Agency or any state administrative agency whose budget, personnel, and other operations are subject to the direction and control of the State and Consumer Services Agency. So for example, if you leave your position at the State and Consumer Services Agency on December 1, 2010, the one-year ban will be in effect until December 1, 2011.

2. Similarly, after your departure from the Department of Finance, the one-year ban applied and you may not, for one year after leaving that position, act for compensation as representative, agent, or attorney for any person before the Department of Finance, the boards, commissions or authorities on which you sat representing for the Director of the Department of Finance, or any state administrative agency whose budget, personnel, and other operations are subject to the direction and control of the Department of Finance.2 Because you left your position at the Department of Finance on January 4, 2010, this one-year ban restriction is in effect until January 4, 2011.

3. Appearances before the Office of the Governor or the Legislature to influence the actions of the Governor or the Legislature on legislation are generally not prohibited because, as Undersecretary for the State and Consumer Services Agency, and Chief Deputy Director for Policy at the Department of Finance, you did not work for the Office of the Governor or the Legislature.

You are currently a Governor's appointee at the State and Consumer Services Agency (Undersecretary) but previously held the Chief Deputy Director position at the California Department of Finance and Deputy Director before that.

Your appointments chronology is as follows:

• Appointed Deputy Director for Legislation, Department of Finance December 23, 2006.
• Appointed Chief Deputy Director for Policy at Department of Finance October 1, 2008.
• Appointed Undersecretary at the State and Consumer Services Agency January 4, 2010.

2 For individuals moving from one state agency to another, a special provision applies. Section 87406(e)(1) states that the prohibitions of the one-year ban do not apply to any individual who is or becomes an "officer or employee of another state agency, board, or commission if the appearance or communication is for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action on behalf of the state agency, board, or commission."

In your role as Chief Deputy at the Department of Finance, which you left on January 4, 2010, you represented the Director of Finance on over 40 boards, commissions and authorities.

In your current role as State and Consumer Services Agency Undersecretary you oversee 14 different departments, commissions and offices that make up the State and Consumer Services Agency. sections.analysis:
There are two provisions in the Act that may restrict your post-governmental employment. In addition, the Act contains a provision about negotiating prospective employment.

Negotiating Prospective Employment

The Act prohibits current state officials from taking part in decisions that directly relate to a prospective employer. Under Section 87407, no public official shall make, participate in making, or influence a governmental decision that directly relates to a prospective employer while the official is negotiating or has reached an employment arrangement with that person. (Section 87407, Regulation 18407.) The Act’s provisions concerning negotiating prospective employment are discussed further in the enclosed fact sheet.

The Permanent Ban

The permanent ban prohibits a state administrative official who leaves state employment from “switching sides” in a specific proceeding that he or she worked on while in state service.

Sections 87401 and 87402 provide:

“No former state administrative official, after the termination of his or her employment or term of office, shall for compensation act as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person (other than the State of California) before any court or state administrative agency or any officer or employee thereof by making any formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or written communication with the intent to influence, in connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding3 if both of the following apply:

3 Section 87400(c) provides: ‘Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding’ means any proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agency, including but not limited to any proceeding governed by Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 11500) of Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government Code.

"(a) The State of California is a party or has a direct and substantial interest.

"(b) The proceeding is one in which the former state administrative official participated.4

"No former state administrative official, after the termination of his or her employment or term of office shall for compensation aid, advise, counsel, consult or assist in representing any other person (except the State of California) in any proceeding in which the official would be prohibited from appearing under Section 87401."

The State and Consumer Services Agency and the Department of Finance are "state administrative agenc[ies]." (Section 87400(a).) According to Section 87400(b), as an officer of the State and Consumer Services Agency and the Department of Finance in a position that had decision-making authority, you are a "state administrative official." Consequently, Sections 87401 and 87402 will prohibit you from representing another person before a court or administrative agency or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting or assisting any other person for compensation in a judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding where the proceeding is one in which you participated; and, the state is a party or has a direct and substantial interest.

You have not asked about any specific proceedings in which you participated at the State and Consumer Services Agency or the Department of Finance. Therefore, we do not analyze the permanent ban in more detail. If you have further questions about specific proceedings, feel free to contact us.

One-Year Ban

The one-year ban prohibits an official from appearing before the agency for which he or she worked for twelve months after leaving state employment. (Section 87406 and Regulations 18746.1 -18746.4, copies enclosed.)

Section 87406(d)(1), provides in relevant part:

"No designated employee of a state administrative agency, any officer, employee, or consultant of a state administrative agency who holds a position which entails the making, or participation in the making, of decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest, and no member of a state administrative agency, for a period of one year after leaving office or employment, shall, for compensation, act as agent or attorney for, or otherwise

4 Section 87400(d) provides: "Participated" means to have taken part personally and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation or use of confidential information as an officer or employee, but excluding approval, disapproval or rendering of legal advisory opinions to departmental or agency staff which do not involve a specific party or parties.

represent, any other person, by making any formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or written communication, before any state administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, for which he or she worked or represented during the 12 months before leaving office or employment, if the appearance or communication is made for the purpose of influencing administrative\(^5\) or legislative action,\(^6\) or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property."

"Influencing legislative or administrative action" includes influencing by any means, including but not limited to the provision or use of information, statistics, studies, or analyses. (Section 82032.)

State Administrative Agency You Worked for or Represented

When you leave your position as Undersecretary of the State and Consumer Services Agency, pursuant to Section 87406(d) you may not act for compensation as a representative, agent, or attorney for any person before the State and Consumer Services Agency for twelve months, or before the Department of Finance while that one-year ban is in effect.

Regulation 18746.1(b)(6) provides that the prohibitions of the one-year ban apply to appearances or communications:

"(C) Made by any former official as specified in subdivision (a)(3)-(a)(5) before any of the following:

"(i) The state administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, that the public official worked for or represented as specified in subdivision (a)(3)-(a)(5). An employee loaned to an agency is deemed to have worked for or represented that agency.

"(ii) Any state administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, whose budget, personnel, and other operations are subject to the direction and control of any agency described in subdivision (b)(6)(C)(i). However, whether an agency is provided technical assistance or legal advice, or is subject to oversight by another

\(^5\) "Administrative action" is defined in Section 82002 as the proposal, drafting, development, consideration, amendment, enactment, or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation or other action in any rate-making proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding.

\(^6\) For purposes of the prohibition on influencing your former employer's "legislative action," this would apply to attempts to influence the former employer's action in connection with their position on the drafting, introduction, consideration, modification, enactment, or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by a member or employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity. "Legislative action" also means the action of the Governor in approving or vetoing any bill. (Section 82037)

agency pursuant to state law, are not factors to be considered in determining whether an agency is subject to the direction and control of another."

Thus, generally, a public official's state administrative agency means the agency for which he or she worked, or any board or commission under that agency's control. However, we have advised that Section 87406 does not prohibit every former member of the executive branch from appearing before or communicating with the entire executive branch. For example, in the Gould Advice Letter, No. A-96-077, we advised that a former director of the Department of Finance, a distinct state administrative agency, may lobby administrative agencies in the executive branch which he did not work for during the course of his government service. In the course of his employment, we concluded that he participated in the budget process as an advisor to the Governor and the Legislature, but he did not work for or represent those agencies within the meaning of Section 87406(d)(1). In making a determination as to which agency an employee works for or represents, the scope of his or her duties is an important factor.

For ex-employees of the Governor's Office, the restrictions are much broader. The statute provides that the "state administrative agency of a designated employee of the Governor's office includes any state administrative agency subject to the direction and control of the Governor." (Section 87406(d)(2).) Following Section 87406(d)(2), for ex-employees of the Governor's Office, we have advised that the "entire executive branch" is subject to the direction and control of the Governor. (Davidian Advice Letter, No. A-97-171; Zaramberg Advice Letter, No. A-92-038; and Sybert Advice Letter, No. I-93-380.)

In your situation, the one-year ban will apply to the State and Consumer Services Agency and the departments, commissions, and offices whose budget, personnel and other operations are subject to the control of the State and Consumer Services Agency. The ban on appearances will apply for one year after you leave the State and Consumer Services Agency.

You note that as State and Consumer Services Agency Undersecretary you oversee 14 different departments, commissions and offices that make up the State and Consumer Services Agency.7 You state that in the case of some of these entities, the State and Consumer Services Agency exerts direct control over some operations, but in the case of other entities, the State and Consumer Services Agency does not exert any direct management control over budgets or personnel transactions. The one-year ban on appearances will apply to those agencies whose budget, personnel and other operations are subject to the control of State and Consumer Services Agency, but not to those who have their own budgets allocated by the Legislature or are

7 Included under the umbrella of the State and Consumer Services Agency are the following departments, commissions, boards and offices: Department of Fair Employment and Housing, Department of Consumer Affairs, Department of General Services, California Building Standards Commission, Fair Employment and Housing Commission, Seismic Safety Commission, California Public Employee Retirement System, California State Teachers' Retirement System, Franchise Tax Board, Victim Compensation and Government Claims Board, State Personnel Board, California Science Center, Exposition Park, Office of Privacy Protection, California African American Museum and the Office of the Insurance Advisor.

independently operated by independent managers (executive director and appointed chairman and commissioners). (Brown Advice Letter, No. A-08-062.)

In addition, the one-year ban on appearing before the Department of Finance runs for one year after your departure from that agency. With respect to the Department of Finance, you are considered to have worked for the boards, commissions and authorities on which you sat representing the Director of the Department of Finance.

Also, generally, a designated employee of a state administrative agency is not restricted by Section 87406 from lobbying the Legislature or Governor regarding legislation. (Davidian Advice Letter, No. A-97-531; Witherspoon Advice Letter, No. A-94-371; and Craven Advice Letter, No. A-93-057.) In your situation, appearances before the Office of the Governor or the Legislature to influence legislation are generally not prohibited because, as Undersecretary for the State and Consumer Services Agency, and Chief Deputy Director for Policy at the Department of Finance, you did not work for the Office of the Governor or the Legislature.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. You wish to confirm your understanding that you are barred from lobbying or otherwise seeking to influence government decisions at the State and Consumer Services Agency and any departments, commissions or authorities under its direct budgetary, personnel and operations control, for one year after the date you leave employment in this position.

2. You wish to confirm your understanding that you are barred from lobbying or otherwise seeking to influence government decisions at the Department of Finance and any boards, commissions or authorities you sat on for one year after the date you left employment in this position.

3. You wish to confirm your belief that you do not have a one-year restriction from lobbying the Governor’s Office or California Legislature on legislative matters.

CONCLUSION: 1. Your understanding is correct. When you leave your position as Undersecretary of the State and Consumer Services Agency, you may not, for one year after leaving state service, act for compensation as representative, agent, or attorney for any person before the State and Consumer Services Agency or any state administrative agency whose budget, personnel, and other operations are subject to the direction and control of the State and Consumer Services Agency. So for example, if you leave your position at the State and Consumer Services Agency on December 1, 2010, the one-year ban will be in effect until December 1, 2011.

2. Similarly, after your departure from the Department of Finance, the one-year ban applied and you may not, for one year after leaving that position, act for compensation as representative, agent, or attorney for any person before the Department of Finance, the boards, commissions or authorities on which you sat representing for the Director of the Department of Finance, or any state administrative agency whose budget, personnel, and other operations are subject to the direction and control of the Department of Finance.2 Because you left your position at the Department of Finance on January 4, 2010, this one-year ban restriction is in effect until January 4, 2011.

3. Appearances before the Office of the Governor or the Legislature to influence the actions of the Governor or the Legislature on legislation are generally not prohibited because, as Undersecretary for the State and Consumer Services Agency, and Chief Deputy Director for Policy at the Department of Finance, you did not work for the Office of the Governor or the Legislature.
embedding.first_500_words: October 25, 2010 Mr. Tom Sheehy 1946 Wingfield Way Carmichael, CA 95608 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-10-155 Dear Mr. Sheehy: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-government employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).1 We note that this advice is based on the facts provided; The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTIONS 1. You wish to confirm your understanding that you are barred from lobbying or otherwise seeking to influence government decisions at the State and Consumer Services Agency and any departments, commissions or authorities under its direct budgetary, personnel and operations control, for one year after the date you leave employment in this position. 2. You wish to confirm your understanding that you are barred from lobbying or otherwise seeking to influence government decisions at the Department of Finance and any boards, commissions or authorities you sat on for one year after the date you left employment in this position. 3. You wish to confirm your belief that you do not have a one-year restriction from lobbying the Governor’s Office or California Legislature on legislative matters. 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. CONCLUSIONS 1. Your understanding is correct. When you leave your position as Undersecretary of the State and Consumer Services Agency, you may not, for one year after leaving state service, act for compensation as representative, agent, or attorney for any person before the State and Consumer Services Agency or any state administrative agency whose budget, personnel, and other operations are subject to the direction and control of the State and Consumer Services Agency. So for example, if you leave your position at the State and Consumer Services Agency on December 1, 2010, the one-year ban will be in effect until December 1, 2011. 2. Similarly, after your departure from the Department of Finance, the one-year ban applied and you may not, for one year after leaving that position, act for compensation as representative, agent, or attorney for any person before the Department of Finance, the boards, commissions or authorities on which you sat representing for the Director of the Department of Finance, or any state administrative agency whose budget, personnel, and other operations are subject to the direction and control of the Department of Finance.2 Because you left your position at the Department of Finance on January 4, 2010, this one-year ban restriction is in effect until January 4, 2011. 3. Appearances before the Office of the Governor or the Legislature to influence the actions of the Governor
